author: @beautifulsmall
text: Interesting investigation. Around 10 yrs ago I tried using an ADNS???  to measure belt position on a balancing robot but found the repeatable accuracy very poor. I contacted the manufacturer at the time and they replied that accuracy was at best 10% but the hand eye co-ordination gives the impression of it being much better. You can test this by running a mouse along an edge to a corner and see where the pointer ends up. Maybe modern mice are better or reading with a non windows interrupted system doesn't miss data. But the one I have isn't.  Its also very dependant on surface roughness as the laser is shon at an angle and the sensor tracks "shadows" .Like a plane flying over mountain ranges I heard was an inspiration.  Maybe P2000 emery cloth would match the sensors resolution ? Best of luck Rob, looking forward to your  results.

replies.author: @transistordave
replies.text: I had a similar project in mind at one time, an industrial application, and had the same conversation with the manufacturer. The repeatability is too poor for accuracy at almost any scale. I don't know if he'll be able to get proper accuracy for 3d printing.

replies.author: @ericthecyclist
replies.text: I've been building a CNC using a module that has a hall-effect device and a magnet you glue to the shaft of your stepper motor. It gives you the position of the stepper. It doesn't give you the absolute position of the spindle, but it does warn you when the spindle hasn't moved to the location you expected. I glued the magnet to a plastic shaft that was in turn glued to the stepper shaft to greater isolate the module+magnet from the stepper motors own fields.

author: @steini19o4
text:
One thing to remember: These optical mice aren't designed to be accurate and only return a rough measurement.

Instead I recommend looking into rotary inductive encoders. These can be built with just clever winding patterns on each of the 3d printed parts, can be made to return an absolute position rather than a relative one and can be made to be incredibly accurate, to the point you can watch your 3d print deforming in real time through room temperature and humidity changes.

author: @GabrielPettier
text:
And 7 pounds is not even a very cheap one, you can find some for about 1, though there are risks with going to the absolute bottom of the barrel, you wouldn't want things to stop working because the electronic is unreliable in the middle of a print.

But yeah, it's incredible the things we take for granted, there is a whole image processing thing in that camera, that identify features of the surface it's filming, and look for them from frame to frame, to compute relative position, and doing that at rates high enough that even on the small surface it's looking at, fast motions can be assessed with some degree of accuracy.

Arduino is certainly a fun and easy way to get into programing with electronics :) (i mean, it's still c++, not the easiest language, but they made it easy for the kinds of things you want to do with these kinds of projects).

edit: "**any** point in space" made me laugh, a very powerful device indeed!

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
Nice video, Robert.  I'm thinking about this as feedback for an automated threading attachment on my lathe.  I have rotary encoders available, but if you use encoders on a leadscrew you get problems with backlash - it's a far better idea to go with direct motion sensing.  I was going to use a linear encoder pulled from a junked inkjet printer, but that's gonna have issues with chips, this is non-contact and can be pretty much sealed.  Bingo!  

As an aside, in general, it's a really bad idea to do any sort of automated control "open loop" - even with steppers driving gears or toothed belts you will eventually get missed steps at the motor end either due to speed, load, or electrical noise issues, and that can really bugger up your day.  Ask anyone trying to run 3d printers as fast as possible.  Even steppers really need to be run servo.  So having a cheap and relatively accurate way of getting actual motion feedback is a really cool thing.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Wow... this was a fantastic jump into electronics. I am truly astounded at how easily you can change subjects! And this is such a cool project to start out with too.
Please be aware that this method uses "relative" mouse position coordinated over time, and will not be accurate in the long term, especially because the number of positional readings has to be read frequently, and it would mean that the arduino would have to be able to keep up with the report rate.  However, you might be able to use some strategies to zero out the position sensor periodically. If you could figure out a way to "home" it every turn for example... that would help.
If you really want more accuracy, (for position), then digital encoders or lasers that can provide "actual distance" might be a better choice.
However, it would be useful for "detecting" slip and could possibly keep up with short adjustments, it is hard to tell, a lot depends on how well the Arduino can keep up with the updates.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are right mate - it hard to tell and 'homing' was the option i thought about

replies.author: @yeabutwecouldbefreer
replies.text: It actually still is open loop because mice sensors only read delta not absolute positions. You could go with replacing the encoder chip on the mouse with an Arduino and depending on the resolution of the camera sensor have it read encoded marks on the axis. Feasibility is another matter.

replies.author: @GabrielPettier
replies.text: @yeabutwecouldbefreer i think you could use the button to make a connection at one point of the rotation, providing you with a reset point to look for at the startup sequence, and useful as a regular error correction during operation, avoiding drift. You can do the integration of deltas into absolute position on the controller easily enough.

author: @TheMrReactorcore
text:
Really interesting project! 
Would the accuracy error accumulate over time as the printer is running? That is, will the total accuray error be added to each time the printer moves? 

Not related, but i was watching your videos on the FWG battery, and i'm loving the science. Did you ever do a "Last status on the battery"-video?

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: Yes, because this uses "relative" positioning, it will be subject to error over time, and that will accumulate unless there is some cool way to "reposition" or zero the sensor. (Also the Arduino has to be capable to read the mouse output at the speed it is sending).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: marc is spot on mate

replies.author: @LeonKeyser
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I think the read rate is not that important. Going thru the code in the library - it looks like the mouse internal processor keeps count of XYZ movement from the last read state. According to the flow in the code, a “pause” state is requested from the client to the mouse and it will only start reading data if the mouse acknowledges. Only then data is read. That is why you would get differential values of XYZ data at each read per clock cycle. 
I could be wrong, but that is my understanding of the C code library. 
You could also inject a custom home GCode XY command in your 3D script to reset that home position every 3-4 layers with that trigger you mentioned. Like what they guys use to snap a photo with a camera each time the bed is at a custom location to trigger a micro switch or something like that.
Like always, loving your content and creativity. 😊
